blocks.text: Re: Utility Relocation Design for Franklin-Snyder
Dear Mr. Green:

This letter sets out the terms on which Brackenridge Civil Group Inc will provide engineering design services to {{fact:f:E-2006-001.client}} in connection with the relocation of utilities within the Franklin-Snyder corridor. It follows our meeting in this office and the correspondence since, and it is intended to serve as the agreement between us. If it states our understanding correctly, please sign and return the enclosed copy at your convenience. We will not commence work until we have the executed copy in hand.
Scope of Services

We will prepare relocation design for the water, sanitary sewer, and dry utility facilities that conflict with the improvements as they are presently shown. The work is divided into three parts. First, we will assemble existing conditions: record drawings from the owners of the affected facilities, the survey furnished to us, and a field review by our staff to confirm what is actually in the ground against what the records show. Second, we will prepare relocation layouts and profiles, including plan and profile sheets, connection details, and the erosion and sediment control measures the relocations require. Third, we will prepare the drawings, technical specifications, and quantity estimate suitable for permit submittal and for bidding.

Our services do not include construction staking, geotechnical investigation, subsurface utility engineering by vacuum excavation, environmental permitting beyond the erosion and sediment control plan, easement plats, or services during construction. Any of these can be added by written amendment, and we will quote them separately if you want them.
Approach

We work from record information first and confirm it in the field before we draw. In our experience the conflicts that cost money on a relocation are the ones nobody knew about at design, and the cheapest hour on a project like this one is the hour spent walking the corridor with the record drawings in hand. Where the records and the field disagree we will tell you which we have relied on and why.

We will coordinate directly with each facility owner as their requirements govern the relocation of their own plant. We will keep you informed of that correspondence, and we will bring anything to you that carries a cost or schedule consequence rather than resolving it on our own. Drawings will be issued to you at the milestones described above for your review, and we will meet with your staff at each issue if you wish.
Project Team

I will serve as Principal Engineer and will seal the drawings. Linda George, Project Engineer, will have day to day charge of the design and will be your working contact; she has run the stormwater and utility design on the great majority of the corridor work this office has produced, and she is the person to call when you have a question about the drawings. Jason Jordan, Design Technician, will prepare the plan set and maintain our drawing standards. The three of us will do the work; we do not subcontract design and we do not assign staff you have not been told about.
Fee

For the scope described above we propose a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2006-001.fee}}, inclusive of our ordinary reimbursable expenses for printing, plotting, delivery, and mileage. We are prepared to begin on {{fact:f:E-2006-001.start}} and to carry the work through to the permit and bid submittal in approximately three months from that date, subject to the timeliness of the record information we receive from the facility owners and of your reviews.
Fees and Payment

We will invoice monthly for the portion of the work completed in the preceding month. Invoices are due thirty days from the date of invoice. Amounts not paid within sixty days will bear interest at one percent per month. Additional services requested in writing and not included in the scope above will be invoiced at our standard hourly rates then in effect, and we will provide an estimate before beginning any such work.
Term and Termination

This agreement remains in effect until the services described above are complete or until it is terminated. Either party may terminate on seven days written notice, with or without cause. Upon termination we will be paid for services performed and expenses incurred through the effective date of termination, and we will deliver to you the work then in progress in the condition it stands.
Confidentiality

We will hold in confidence the information you furnish to us that is marked or identified as confidential, and we will not disclose it except to our own staff, to the facility owners and reviewing agencies to the extent required to perform the work, or as required by law. This obligation does not extend to information already in the public record, information already known to us, or information disclosed to us by a third party without restriction.
Limitation of Liability

Our services will be performed with the care and skill ordinarily exercised by members of our profession practicing under similar conditions in this locality. No other warranty, express or implied, is made. To the fullest extent permitted by law, our aggregate liability to you arising out of this agreement, whether in contract, tort, or otherwise, is limited to the amount of the fee stated above, and neither party is liable to the other for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages, including loss of use or loss of profit.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which the project is located. Any claim arising under it will be brought in the courts of the county in which the project is located. This letter, with any written amendments signed by both of us, is the entire agreement between us and supersedes our prior discussions and correspondence on the subject.

If the foregoing is acceptable, please sign both copies where indicated, retain one for your file, and return the other to this office. Should any part of it require adjustment before you can sign, call me and we will take it up directly. We appreciate the confidence {{fact:f:E-2006-001.client}} has placed in this firm and we will do the work carefully.
Very truly yours,
Memorandum

To: Jason Jordan, Design Technician
From: Linda George, Project Engineer
Re: Utility Relocation Design for Franklin-Snyder, project kickoff
cc: Mark Todd, Principal Engineer

The agreement is executed and we start on {{fact:f:E-2006-001.start}}. What we owe {{fact:f:E-2006-001.client}} at the end is a permit and bid set for the relocations in the Franklin-Snyder corridor: plan and profile, details, specifications, and quantities. Everything below serves that. The schedule is three months and it is not generous, so the order of work matters more than usual.

The objective is narrow and worth stating plainly, because a relocation job drifts if it is not. We are not redesigning the corridor and we are not solving the client's easement problems. We are moving three owners' facilities out of the way of improvements that are already laid out, drawing the result to each owner's own standards, and handing over a set a contractor can bid without calling us. Anything that does not serve that is not in the fee.
Workstreams

items: Records and existing conditions. Request record drawings from each facility owner in the first week. These take longer to arrive than anyone expects, so the requests go out before we draw anything. Log what we asked for and the date, and log what comes back.

Field review. Once the survey is loaded, we walk the corridor against the records and mark what does not match. I want us both out there for the first pass. Anything we cannot confirm from the surface gets flagged on the drawings as unverified rather than shown as fact.
Base sheet setup. Sheet borders, layer standards, and the survey control set up once, correctly, before layout begins. Jason Jordan owns this and nobody else moves a layer.
Relocation layout and profiles. Water first, since the conflicts there drive the sanitary alignment, then sanitary, then dry utilities. Profiles as we go, not at the end.
Erosion and sediment control. My work. It follows the layout and I do not want it started until the alignments have settled.
Specifications and quantities. Assembled from the drawings at the back end, but the specification sections we know we need should be pulled and listed now.
Owner coordination. Each facility owner has its own standards for its own plant and we conform to them. Their comments come to me.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: This week: records requests out to all facility owners; I will draft the letters and Jason Jordan will assemble the exhibit for each.
This week: survey loaded and checked against control. Report any discrepancy to me before you build on it.
Next week: field review, both of us, one day if the weather holds.
Following week: base sheets complete and the first water alignment in progress.
Progress meeting with Mark Green and his staff once the field review is done, so we go into that meeting with something to say. I will schedule it.

blocks.text: Two things to keep in front of you. First, anything that comes back from a facility owner that changes scope, cost, or schedule goes to Mark Todd and to me before it goes anywhere else; it is not ours to absorb quietly. Second, this is a fixed fee, so hours we spend redrawing what we drew wrong come out of the firm. Get the records in, confirm them in the field, then draw. In that order.

One administrative note. I keep my own notes of every meeting with the client and the facility owners, in addition to whatever minutes are taken. That is not a comment on anyone's minutes. It is that a recollection differing from mine once cost this office a week, and it will not do so again.
Ask me if anything here is unclear. I would rather answer a question now than find the answer on a sheet later.
Minutes of Progress Meeting

Project: Utility Relocation Design for Franklin-Snyder
Client: {{fact:f:E-2006-001.client}}
Location: Offices of Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Minutes taken by: Jason Jordan, Design Technician
Attendees

items: Linda George, Project Engineer, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Jason Jordan, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Mark Green, Chief Financial Officer, Franklin-Snyder

blocks.text: Discussion

Linda George reviewed the status of the design. The water relocation is laid out through the corridor and the profiles are drawn. Sanitary layout is under way. Dry utility layout has not started and is not scheduled to start until the sanitary alignment is fixed. The erosion and sediment control plan follows both and has not been started.

Linda George reported one conflict of consequence. The record drawings for a segment of the existing water main show it at a depth and alignment that the field review did not confirm. The facility owner has been asked for any later as-built information. If none exists, the alignment as drawn is based on the field review and is noted on the sheets as unverified. Mark Green asked what that means for the bid. Linda George stated that it means the contractor bids the relocation as drawn and that a differing condition in that segment is a change, and that the alternative is test pits before the set is issued, which are not in our scope and would have to be arranged and paid for by others.

Mark Green asked whether test pits could be added and what they would cost. Linda George stated that we do not perform them, that we would specify the locations, and that the cost and the schedule for them would come from whoever is engaged to dig. Mark Green asked for the locations and an estimate of the delay so that his side can decide. He did not commit to the work.

Two facility owners have returned record drawings. One has not. Linda George stated that the third owner's plant is at the end of the corridor and that its absence does not hold up the water or sanitary work, but that it will hold up the dry utility layout if the records are not in hand by the time that layout begins. Mark Green stated that he would make a call on the firm's behalf. Nobody objected to that.

The undersigned raised the drawing standards for the plan set. The set will be issued under a sealed cover sheet and will conform to this office's standards, not to any contractor markup received in the interim. Mark Green had no comment.
Next progress meeting to be scheduled by Linda George after the sanitary layout is complete.
Action Items

rows: Provide proposed test pit locations and an estimate of the schedule impact
Call the third facility owner regarding outstanding record drawings
Complete sanitary relocation layout and profiles
Annotate the unverified water main segment on the plan and profile sheets
Hold dry utility layout pending receipt of the outstanding records
Advise this office in writing whether test pits will be performed

blocks.text: These minutes are our record of the meeting. Please advise this office within five days of any correction; otherwise they will stand as written and go in the job file.
Progress Report: Utility Relocation Design for Franklin-Snyder
Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2006-001.client}} by Linda George, Project Engineer
Summary

The design is on schedule and on budget. We are at roughly the midpoint of the work and roughly the midpoint of the fee. The water relocation design is complete through profiles. The sanitary relocation layout is drawn and its profiles are in progress. The dry utility layout has not begun and is waiting on record drawings from one facility owner. There is one open item that could affect the bid, described under Risks below, and it is not ours to close.

The short version for anyone reading only this section: nothing here requires action from your side except a decision on the test pits, and that decision has a date attached to it. Everything else is running the way we said it would run.
Accomplishments This Period

items: Record drawings received from two of the three affected facility owners and reconciled against the survey.
Field review of the corridor completed. Eleven crossings confirmed against the records; one segment of existing water main was not confirmed and is discussed below.
Water relocation plan and profile complete, including connection details at both ends.
Sanitary relocation layout complete; profiles approximately half drawn.

Base sheets, borders, and drawing standards established for the full set. Jason Jordan has the set built so that the remaining sheets drop into a structure that already exists, which is why the second half of a job like this goes faster than the first.
Progress meeting held with your staff at this office. Minutes issued separately.

blocks.text: Upcoming Work

items: Complete sanitary relocation profiles.
Begin dry utility layout on receipt of the outstanding record drawings.
Prepare the erosion and sediment control plan once the alignments are fixed.
Assemble technical specifications and the quantity estimate.
Internal review and seal, then issue the permit and bid set.

blocks.text: The sequence above is not arbitrary. The sanitary alignment is governed by the water relocation, the dry utility layout is governed by both, and the erosion and sediment control plan is governed by all three. Drawing any of them out of order means drawing them twice. Jason Jordan and I have held the dry utility sheets empty rather than populate them from records we do not have, and that is deliberate; empty sheets are honest and a set built on assumed plant is not.
Budget

The engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2006-001.fee}} and we do not expect to exceed the scope it covers. Our effort to date is consistent with the portion of the work completed, and we have invoiced accordingly. Nothing described in this report is an extra. Should the test pits discussed below be authorized, that work would be performed and paid for by others; our part would be to specify the locations and to incorporate the results, which we can absorb within the fee if the results arrive before the set is assembled.
Risks

The unverified water main segment is the item that matters. The records place it at one depth and alignment; the field review could not confirm it and the facility owner has produced nothing later. As things stand we will draw it as our field review indicates and note it on the sheets as unverified, which means a differing condition there becomes a change order during construction. Test pits before we issue would settle it. That decision, and the cost of it, sits with your side, and we have provided the locations. We do not need an answer today, but we do need one before the set is assembled, or the note stands.

The second risk is the outstanding record drawings for the third facility owner. The water and sanitary work does not depend on them. The dry utility layout does. If those drawings are not in hand by the time we reach that layout, the schedule slips by about the length of the delay, and there is no way to work around it that does not amount to guessing at another owner's plant, which we will not do.

The third risk is ordinary and I mention it only so it is on the record: review comments from a facility owner that arrive late and require an alignment change cost more the later they arrive. We have asked each owner for comments as we go rather than at the end. Two are cooperating.

None of the three risks is presently affecting the schedule or the fee, and I am not asking for relief on either. I am reporting them at the midpoint because the midpoint is when they can still be managed cheaply. The first one has a decision behind it, the second has a telephone call behind it, and the third has nothing behind it but attention.

Franklin-Snyder should expect the next report at the completion of the sanitary profiles. Call me with any question on the above; I would rather take the question than have this report answer one you did not ask.
